stem: Which of the following is guaranteed to be true when the `else` block of an `if`/`else` statement executes?

options.label: A
options.text: The boolean expression in the `if` condition evaluated to `true`

options.label: B
options.text: The boolean expression in the `if` condition evaluated to `false`

options.label: C
options.text: Both the `if` block and the `else` block execute during the same run

options.label: D
options.text: The `if` condition must contain a relational operator

explanation: The else block executes if and only if the if condition is false. Option A is the opposite. Option C is impossible; in an if/else exactly one branch runs. Option D is incorrect because any boolean expression — even a literal `false` — can serve as the condition.

5 > 10 is false, so the else branch executes: y = 10 − 5 = 5. x is unchanged at 5. Option A results from mistakenly taking the if branch. Option B assumes neither branch runs. Option D assumes both branches run, which is impossible in an if/else.

options.label: C
options.text: A compile error occurs because the `else` has no matching `if`

explanation: Without braces, the if controls only the single statement `System.out.println("hot")`. The next line `System.out.println("wear sunscreen")` is a standalone statement, leaving the following `else` with no matching `if`, which is a compile error. Braces are required to group multiple statements into an if body.

8 > 5 is true, so the outer if body executes. Then 8 % 2 == 0 is true, so the inner if prints "even winner". Option B would require a to be odd. Option C would require a ≤ b. Option D is impossible because in an if/else exactly one branch runs.

The else pairs with the nearest unmatched if, which is `if (x > 20)`. With x = 15, the outer if (x > 10) is true, so the inner if/else is entered; x > 20 is false, printing "medium". `System.out.print(" done")` is outside both ifs and always executes. Option C would require x ≤ 10.

options.label: C
options.text: An `ArithmeticException` is thrown at runtime due to division by zero

explanation: With x = 0, `x != 0` evaluates to false. Due to short-circuit evaluation of &&, the right operand `10 / x` is never evaluated, so no division by zero occurs. The condition is false and the else branch prints "no". Option C is the classic trap for students who do not know short-circuit evaluation prevents the division.

79 ≥ 90 is false, 79 ≥ 80 is false, and 79 ≥ 70 is true, so grade is set to "C" and the remaining else is skipped. In an else-if chain at most one branch executes — the first whose condition is true. Option B would require score ≥ 80; option D would require score < 70.

Both conditions are satisfied for n = 6 (6 % 2 == 0 and 6 % 3 == 0), so both if blocks execute: result becomes false, then immediately becomes true. These are two independent if statements, not an if/else, so both always run. Option B is the trap for students who mentally treat the second if as an else if and skip it.

options.label: A
options.text: `positive` and `done` are printed; `nonzero` prints only when `x > 0`

options.label: D
options.text: A compile error occurs because braces are missing

explanation: Without braces, the if controls only `System.out.println("positive")`. Both `System.out.println("nonzero")` and `System.out.println("done")` are unconditional statements that always execute. Missing braces cause a logic bug, not a compile error (ruling out D). With x = 3, all three lines print.

p * 2 = 8 equals q = 8, so the outer if body runs: p becomes 5, q becomes 7. Then 5 > 7 is false, printing "Y". Finally p + q = 5 + 7 = 12 prints as an integer. Option A would require p > q after the updates (5 is not > 7). Option C would require p * 2 ≠ q. Option D has an arithmetic error on the sum.

s1 is a string literal while s2 is created with `new String()`, so they are distinct objects; `s1 == s2` compares references and is false, printing "diff ref". However, both contain the same characters, so `s1.equals(s2)` returns true, printing " same val". Always use `.equals()` to compare String content; `==` tests reference identity.

18 % 3 evaluates to 0, so n % 3 == 0 is true and the if-branch executes, printing "fizz"; the else-branch is skipped. B would print if the condition were false. C and D confuse printing the result of the modulo operation or the variable n with the string literals in the branches.

First if: p > q → 8 > 4 is true, so p = 8 - 4 = 4. Second if: p == q → 4 == 4 is true, so q = 0. Final: p = 4, q = 0. A correctly traces the first if but fails to execute the second if. C takes the else branch (p = 8 + 4 = 12) and skips the second if (12 == 4 is false). D takes the else branch but incorrectly assumes 12 == 4 triggers q = 0.

Without braces, only the immediately following statement belongs to the if-block. System.out.println("done") is unconditionally executed regardless of the condition. Since x = 2 and 2 > 3 is false, "greater" is not printed, but "done" always prints. A and B incorrectly assume the condition is true; D incorrectly assumes both statements are guarded by the if.

stem: Which of the following correctly describes when the `else` branch of an `if`/`else` statement executes?

options.label: A
options.text: Every time the `if`/`else` statement is reached during program execution

options.label: B
options.text: Only when the boolean condition evaluates to `true`

options.label: C
options.text: Only when the boolean condition evaluates to `false`

options.label: D
options.text: Only when the `if` branch completes without printing any output

explanation: An if/else statement evaluates its boolean condition and executes exactly one branch: the if-branch when the condition is true, and the else-branch when the condition is false. A is wrong because both branches never run in the same execution. B describes when the if-branch (not the else-branch) runs. D describes no valid Java behavior.

a == b → 4 == 4 is true, so the outer else is skipped. a = 4 * 2 = 8. Now a > b → 8 > 4 is true (b is still 4 at this moment), so b = 4 + 3 = 7. Output: "8 7". B takes the wrong inner branch (b - 3 = 1). C incorrectly takes the outer else (a != b would have to be true). D forgets that a is updated to 8 before the inner condition is evaluated, using the stale value 4.

12 % 5 = 2, not 0, so the first if condition is false and msg = "buzz". Then 12 % 4 = 0, so the second if is true and msg becomes "buzz!". A and C both require 12 % 5 == 0, which is false. B correctly identifies the first branch but ignores that the second if also executes.

Since p == q (both 4), the condition p != q is false and the else branch executes: q = 4 + 4 = 8; p is unchanged at 4. A incorrectly assumes neither branch ran (impossible in an if-else). C reflects the if branch executing (p != q true), which it is not. D assumes both branches ran, which is impossible.

Without braces, only the immediately following statement (println("greater")) belongs to the if block. Since val = 3 is not > 5, that line is skipped. However, println("done") is outside the if and always executes unconditionally, printing "done". D is wrong because the second println is not guarded by the condition.

options.label: C
options.text: An `ArithmeticException` is thrown at runtime because `y / x` divides by zero.

options.label: D
options.text: Nothing is printed because the boolean condition cannot be fully evaluated.

explanation: x = 0, so x != 0 evaluates to false. Short-circuit evaluation of && immediately makes the entire condition false without ever evaluating y / x, so no divide-by-zero occurs. The else branch executes and prints "guarded". C is the classic short-circuit trap: the right operand of && is never reached when the left is false. D is incorrect because the condition resolves normally to false.
Since `x > 5` is false, the nested `if` and its paired `else` are skipped. The final print statement always runs, so the output is `C`.
`18` is divisible by `2` and `3`, so `E` and `T` are added. It is not divisible by `5`, so `R` is added, giving `ETR`.
